critic_raw: The 72-hour clinical deterioration prediction design is well-structured. I want to probe the outcome definition: 'unplanned ICU transfer, rapid response team activation, or in-hospital mortality.' These are three distinct outcomes with different clinical significance. An unplanned ICU transfer may have very different predictors than in-hospital mortality. Combining them into a single binary label may make the model hard to interpret clinically and may produce a model that predicts one outcome well but not others. Additionally, the design excludes 'palliative/comfort-care patients' and 'readmissions within 30 days' — but palliative patients may have similar vital sign patterns to deteriorating patients, and their exclusion might inflate apparent model performance.
defender_raw: On composite outcome: the composite of ICU transfer + RRT activation + mortality is a well-established clinical endpoint in early warning score literature (e.g., MEWS, NEWS). Combining these into a composite outcome is clinically justified because they all represent 'failure to rescue' — the same clinical intervention priority. The design correctly focuses on prediction of 'the need for escalated care' rather than any specific outcome. On exclusion of palliative patients: excluding palliative patients is clinically appropriate — these patients are intentionally not escalated to ICU, so including them would create label noise (their deterioration is intentional). Their exclusion does not inflate performance but rather focuses the model on the clinically relevant population where intervention is appropriate.
adjudication_raw: Two concerns: composite outcome heterogeneity and palliative patient exclusion. Both addressed: composite outcome is clinically justified as 'failure to rescue'; palliative exclusion is clinically appropriate to focus on actionable patients. Verdict: defense_wins.
